sections.paragraph:
(for פַּחָה with Dag. forte implied), m. const. פַּחַת, with suff. פֶּחָֽתְךָ, once פֶּחָם Neh 5:14 (from the masculine form פַּח), pl. פַּחֹות 1Ki 10:15 20:24 Jer 51:23 Eze 23:6, 23 Eze 23:23const. state פַּחֲוֹות (from absol. פֶּחָֹוות) Neh 2:7, Ezr 8:36, m. the governor of a province (less than that of a satrap, see אֲחַשְׁדַּרְפָּן) in the Assyrian empire, 2Ki 18:24 Isa 36:9 Chaldean, Eze 23:6, 23 Eze 23:23 Jer 51:23 Persian, Est 8:9 9:3 specially used of the Persian governor of Judæa, Hag 1:1, 14 Hag 1:14 2:2 , 21 Hag 2:21 Neh 5:14, 18 12:26 Mal 1:8 used of the governor of Judea in the time of Solomon, 1Ki 10:15 and of the governors of Syria, 1Ki 20:24. (This word appears to be of foreign origin, and Bohlen compares Pers. بكيت, بكيتا great men, nobles; Ewald in Gramm. p. 490, the verb جختن pukhten, to care for, to act (pr. to cook, bake; Germ. backen.) Better than all these would be بك, بيك, بكاa prince, leader, commander of Soldiers, were not this apparently a mere Turkish word; but the Persian is بيك paik, one of the guard, an attendant.) [Benfey compares Sanscr. paksha, companion, friend; and this Gesenius appears to have preferred.]

פחה

constr. פַּחַת, pl. emphat. פַּחֲוָתָא, Chald. id. Ezr 5:3, 14 Ezr 5:14 6:7 Dan 3:2, , 27 6:8 .
